title: SEGMENT 7 — The 1996 Bermuda Principles: open data within 24 hours

lines.text: SEGMENT 7 — The 1996 Bermuda Principles: open data within 24 hours
In February 1996, the heads of the major sequencing centers in the international consortium met at a Wellcome Trust strategy meeting at the Princess Sophia Hotel in Hamilton, Bermuda.
The meeting was called to address a practical problem: as sequencing scaled up, sequence data was piling up in individual labs faster than it could be published in papers.
What was the data-sharing policy supposed to be?
The answer they reached — over three days of argument, mostly driven by John Sulston and Robert Waterston, with Michael Morgan of Wellcome chairing — was radical, and it changed biology.
The Bermuda Principles, agreed in February 1996 and reaffirmed at follow-up meetings in 1997 and 1998, stated three things.

One: all human genomic sequence information generated by any center funded by the public consortium would be freely available in the public domain, with no patent restrictions, to encourage research and development and maximize benefit to society.
Two: sequence assemblies of one kilobase or larger would be released to the public databases — GenBank, EMBL, DDBJ — within twenty-four hours of generation.
Three: finished annotated sequences would be submitted immediately upon completion.
Twenty-four hours.
Read that again.
Twenty-four hours from the moment a sequencer spit out a read to the moment that read was in a global public database that anybody on earth could download.
In 1996, this was utterly unprecedented.
The norm in molecular biology, and in science generally, was that you held onto your data until you published your paper.
Publication was the moment of release.
Data generated months or years before publication sat in lab notebooks and local databases, accessible only to the generating lab and its collaborators.
The Bermuda Principles tore that norm apart.
Here is the analogy.
Imagine a world in which every new mathematical theorem had to sit in the prover's desk drawer until a peer-reviewed paper came out three years later.

Now imagine somebody passing a rule that says every new theorem must be posted on a public bulletin board within twenty-four hours of being proven, even before it has been written up, even before the prover has had time to figure out what it means.
That is the size of the cultural shift that Bermuda represented for sequence data.
The argument for the principles was scientific.
Genome sequence is infrastructure.
If everybody has it, everybody can use it to do their own science, and the scientific community as a whole moves faster.
Holding genome data hostage to individual publication timelines would slow down all of biology.
The genome belongs to the species, not to the people who happen to sequence it first.
The argument was also strategic, although nobody said this out loud at the time.
Open data was a weapon against Venter.

If every base the public consortium generated was in GenBank within twenty-four hours, and Celera's data was proprietary and behind a subscription paywall, then the public effort had a moral high ground and a practical scientific value that Celera could not match.
Researchers who wanted to use human sequence in their own work would default to the public data because it was free.
The Bermuda Principles are arguably the single most consequential policy decision of the entire Human Genome Project.
They established that publicly funded genome data is a public good.
They established that immediate release, not publication-gated release, is the right default.

And they set a precedent that has since been extended to other large-scale data: protein structures in the Protein Data Bank, microarray data in GEO, sequencing reads in the Short Read Archive, clinical trial data in ClinicalTrials.gov, COVID-19 genome sequences in GISAID and the public archives.

When you go to GenBank today and download a sequence, you are exercising a right that John Sulston and Robert Waterston and the others fought for in a hotel conference room in Bermuda in February 1996.
That open-data culture is one of the two great cultural legacies of the Human Genome Project.
The other is the ELSI program, which we will get to in Segment 16.
